ps_throughput.motivation: Representative conservative benchmark-level estimate for Hyperledger Fabric under moderate permissioned deployment assumptions. Reported throughput varies substantially depending on endorsement policy, ordering configuration, block parameters, channel structure, chaincode complexity, and hardware allocation; the adopted value is intended as a comparative baseline rather than as a peak-performance claim.
ps_throughput.source: Androulaki et al.; LF Decentralized Trust, Benchmarking Hyperledger Fabric 2.5 Performance

parallel_execution.motivation: Fabric supports parallel transaction proposal, endorsement, and chaincode execution before ordering, even though final commit remains ordered within each channel.

horizontal_partitioning.motivation: Fabric supports horizontal workload and governance partitioning through channels, organisations, peers, and endorsement-policy decomposition.

absence_of_global_serialization.motivation: Fabric does not require a single global ledger order across the entire deployment; ordering is scoped to individual channels rather than imposed as one universal total-order layer for all transactions in the network.

scalable_coordination_model.motivation: Within each channel, write coordination still depends on an ordering service, which remains a structural coordination bottleneck even if multiple channels can coexist in parallel.

ps_scalability.source: Hyperledger Fabric Documentation; Androulaki et al.

eco_baseline_footprint.formula: BIF = (vCPU x 15 EUR) + (Storage GB x 0.08 EUR)
eco_baseline_footprint.method_note: RAM is considered included in compute cost and is not monetized separately in the operationalized framework model.
eco_baseline_footprint.motivation: Calculated on the adopted MCC as (6 x 15) + (100 x 0.08) = 98 EUR/month.
eco_baseline_footprint.source: Framework internal calculation based on mandated MCC

eco_variable_transaction_cost.motivation: Hyperledger Fabric does not impose a protocol-level per-transaction fee or native gas model under self-managed permissioned deployments. In this framework, Variable Transaction Cost captures protocol-level usage-dependent transaction fees rather than infrastructure expenditure.

eco_storage_footprint.motivation: Baseline persistent storage allocation associated with the adopted MCC, used as a normalized comparative baseline rather than as an estimate of long-term ledger growth under a production workload.

append_only_structural_history.motivation: The Fabric ledger includes an append-only blockchain transaction log.

cryptographic_linkage_between_records.motivation: Fabric blocks are cryptographically linked to previous blocks.

cryptographic_write_authentication.motivation: Transactions, endorsements, and identities are authenticated through digital signatures and valid certificates.

independent_integrity_verification.motivation: Peers independently validate transactions and blocks before commit according to endorsement, validation, and access-control policies.

rci_tamper_evidence_auditability.source: Hyperledger Fabric Documentation; Androulaki et al.

protocol_enforced_irreversible_commit.motivation: Fabric provides deterministic commit finality in the sense that committed blocks are not subject to probabilistic fork resolution under the native ordering-and-validation model.

multi_party_confirmation_requirement.motivation: Multi-party endorsement can be required through endorsement policies, but it is configurable and not structurally mandatory for every Fabric deployment or every transaction.

absence_of_unilateral_rollback_capabilit.motivation: Once committed to the append-only ledger, ordinary participants cannot unilaterally roll back committed history under the native protocol model.

rci_finality_rollback_resistance.source: Hyperledger Fabric Documentation; Androulaki et al.

absence_of_mandatory_single_writer_depen.motivation: Write progress depends on the ordering service as a mandatory coordination point in the adopted MCC.

quorum_based_write_continuation.motivation: The adopted MCC uses a single ordering node rather than a quorum-based ordering cluster.

automatic_write_continuation_mechanism.motivation: Automatic ordering-service failover is not available under the adopted MCC.

single_node_failure_survivability_of_wri.motivation: Failure of the single ordering node would interrupt the write path in the benchmark configuration.

rci_write_availability_fault_tolerance.source: Hyperledger Fabric Documentation; adopted MCC definition

absence_of_single_entity_unilateral_stat.motivation: In representative multi-organisation Fabric deployments, no ordinary single participant has unilateral authority over valid ledger state once endorsement, ordering, and validation policies are enforced.

structural_requirement_for_multi_entity_.motivation: Multi-entity validation and endorsement are natively supported, but the requirement for multiple independent entities is policy-dependent and not structurally mandatory in every configuration.

validator_admission_independence.motivation: Participant and validator admission is permissioned and depends on MSPs, certificates, channel configuration, and explicit governance decisions rather than on open protocol-level admission.

absence_of_structurally_fixed_validator_.motivation: Fabric supports reconfigurable organisations, peers, orderers, and policy structures rather than an immutable participant set fixed by protocol.

ags_control_distribution_governance.source: Hyperledger Fabric Documentation, Governance and Ordering Service

native_privilege_differentiation_capabil.motivation: Fabric natively provides privilege differentiation through organisations, MSPs, certificates, administrators, endorsement policies, ACLs, and channel-level policy constructs.

native_identity_lifecycle_governance_cap.motivation: Fabric natively supports identity lifecycle governance through registration, enrolment, renewal, revocation, MSP configuration, and certificate-based membership management.

structural_independence_of_identity_gove.motivation: Identity and access governance are enforced natively through MSPs, certificate validation, endorsement policies, ACLs, and channel configuration rules rather than being delegated entirely to external application logic.

ags_identity_access_governance.source: Hyperledger Fabric CA Documentation; Fabric MSP and ACL model

non_unilateral_compromise_requirement.motivation: In representative multi-organisation Fabric deployments, compromising validation or ledger outcomes is not structurally unilateral for an ordinary participant when endorsement and governance policies involve multiple organisations.

majority_compromise_required.motivation: Fabric does not universally impose a majority-compromise threshold across all relevant validation, endorsement, ordering, and governance decisions; the compromise threshold depends on configured endorsement and governance policies rather than on a protocol-level majority rule.

supermajority_compromise_required.motivation: Fabric does not provide a general protocol-level supermajority compromise threshold comparable to BFT-style or public-chain consensus mechanisms across all deployments.
